title: John Beradino

infobox.value: (born **Giovanni Berardino**, May 1, 1917 – May 19, 1996) was an American [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") [infielder](/wiki/Infielder "Infielder") and actor. Known as **Johnny Berardino**[[2]](#cite_note-LATimes-2) during his baseball career, he was also credited during his acting career as John Berardino, John Baradino, John Barardino or John Barradino.

**John Beradino** (born **Giovanni Berardino**, May 1, 1917 – May 19, 1996) was an American [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") [infielder](/wiki/Infielder "Infielder") and actor. Known as **Johnny Berardino**[[2]](#cite_note-LATimes-2) during his baseball career, he was also credited during his acting career as John Berardino, John Baradino, John Barardino or John Barradino.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg/250px-1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg)

Publicity photo for the 10th Anniversary of *General Hospital*, 1973

Beradino was born in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles")[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) and was raised near [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles").[[2]](#cite_note-LATimes-2) He attended [Belmont High School](/wiki/Belmont_High_School_(Los_Angeles) "Belmont High School (Los Angeles)") in downtown Los Angeles. Beradino won a football scholarship to the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") in 1936,[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) but he soon switched to baseball.[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3)

Although Beradino is sometimes believed to have appeared in the silent *[Our Gang](/wiki/Our_Gang "Our Gang")* comedies as a child actor, he has not been identified as having appeared in any of the existing films.[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Baseball")]

After attending the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California"), where he played baseball under coach [Sam Barry](/wiki/Sam_Barry "Sam Barry") and was member of [Phi Kappa Tau](/wiki/Phi_Kappa_Tau "Phi Kappa Tau") fraternity, Beradino was a [major league](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") player from 1939 to 1952,[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) except for three years of military service in the [U.S. Naval Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve")[[4]](#cite_note-Hard-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) during World War II from 1942 to 1945. He played for the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns"), [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") and [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates"), winning the [World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") with the Indians in 1948.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESOD200967-6) While primarily a middle infielder, playing [second baseman](/wiki/Second_base "Second base") or [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop"), he also played first and third base.

After injuring his leg and being released by Pittsburgh in 1952, he retired from [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") and returned to acting, having appeared in his first film in 1948.

### Acting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Acting")]

Beradino appeared briefly in an uncredited role as a state trooper in the 1954 [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") *[Suddenly](/wiki/Suddenly_(1954_film) "Suddenly (1954 film)")*, starring [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra") and [Sterling Hayden](/wiki/Sterling_Hayden "Sterling Hayden"), and later performed as a policeman who allows Roger Thornhill ([Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant")) to make a phone call to his mother in the 1959 [Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") thriller *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")*.

Beradino (still billed as John Berardino) played a [cameo role](/wiki/Cameo_role "Cameo role") in the 1954 sci-fi thriller *[Them!](/wiki/Them! "Them!")*. He also appeared in a 1956 episode of the [television series](/wiki/Television_series "Television series") *[Adventures of Superman](/wiki/Adventures_of_Superman_(TV_series) "Adventures of Superman (TV series)")* titled "The Unlucky Number" as a small-time criminal struggling to reform.

Beradino appeared twice on the Western series *[Annie Oakley](/wiki/Annie_Oakley_(TV_series) "Annie Oakley (TV series)"):* as Gorman in "Annie Rides the Navajo Trail" and as Roscoe Barnes in "Amateur Outlaw" (both 1956). He appeared as an outlaw in the opening scenes of [Budd Boetticher](/wiki/Budd_Boetticher "Budd Boetticher")'s [*Seven Men From Now*](/wiki/Seven_Men_from_Now "Seven Men from Now") in 1956. He guest-starred on [John Bromfield](/wiki/John_Bromfield "John Bromfield")'s syndicated crime drama with a modern Western setting, *[Sheriff of Cochise](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Cochise "Sheriff of Cochise")*, and Bromfield's successor series, *[U.S. Marshal](/wiki/Sheriff_of_Cochise "Sheriff of Cochise")*. He was also cast in an episode of [David Janssen](/wiki/David_Janssen "David Janssen")'s crime drama series *[Richard Diamond, Private Detective](/wiki/Richard_Diamond,_Private_Detective "Richard Diamond, Private Detective")*.

Beradino played a minor gangster in *[The Untouchables](/wiki/The_Untouchables_(1959_TV_series) "The Untouchables (1959 TV series)")* pilot that originally aired as an installment in the *[Westinghouse Desilu Playhouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Desilu_Playhouse "Westinghouse Desilu Playhouse")*. He then played the recurring role of gangster Augie Viale in two episodes from the first season of *The Untouchables* series, "The Jake Lingle Killing" and "One-Armed Bandits".

On December 2, 1959, Beradino appeared in the episode "The Third Strike" of the [syndicated](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") adventure series *[Rescue 8](/wiki/Rescue_8 "Rescue 8")*, playing a professional baseball player who loses consciousness when struck by a [wild pitch](/wiki/Wild_pitch "Wild pitch") and awakens with short-term [amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia").

After appearing in more than a dozen [B-movies](/wiki/B-movie "B-movie"), as well as in supporting roles on the series *[I Led Three Lives](/wiki/I_Led_Three_Lives "I Led Three Lives")* and *[The New Breed](/wiki/The_New_Breed_(TV_series) "The New Breed (TV series)")*,[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) he was offered the role of Dr. [Steve Hardy](/wiki/Steve_Hardy_(General_Hospital) "Steve Hardy (General Hospital)") on the [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") *[General Hospital](/wiki/General_Hospital "General Hospital")*.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKearneyBuchanan197617–24,_53–55-7) Beradino also played a version of his *General Hospital* character on an episode of *[The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air](/wiki/The_Fresh_Prince_of_Bel-Air "The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air")*.

## Recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recognition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/General_Hospital_Cast_1973.jpg/250px-General_Hospital_Cast_1973.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_Hospital_Cast_1973.jpg)

Cast of [General Hospital](/wiki/General_Hospital "General Hospital") 1973 (top): John Beradino, [Emily McLaughlin](/wiki/Emily_McLaughlin "Emily McLaughlin") (bottom): [Martin West](/wiki/Martin_West_(actor) "Martin West (actor)"), [Rachel Ames](/wiki/Rachel_Ames "Rachel Ames"), [Peter Hansen](/wiki/Peter_Hansen_(actor) "Peter Hansen (actor)")

For his contribution to the television industry, Beradino has a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame")[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) at 6801 Hollywood Blvd. He has also been inducted into the [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") Athletic Hall of Fame.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

He is the only person to have won a [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") (1948) and have his star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") (1993).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Beradino received three [Daytime Emmy Award](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award "Daytime Emmy Award") nominations for best actor in a daytime drama.[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3)

In tribute, *General Hospital* left Beradino's image with that of [Rachel Ames](/wiki/Rachel_Ames "Rachel Ames") in its opening sequence for more than a year after his death, through several updates.[[8]](#cite_note-8) His image was finally removed in early 1998, but an action clip of Beradino's character remained in the sequence until its 2004 retirement.

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Beradino had two children, Toni and Cindy from his first marriage, and two children, Katherine Ann and John Anthony from his second marriage. He was married to his third wife, Marjorie, at the time of his death.[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3)

Beradino was diagnosed with [pancreatic cancer](/wiki/Pancreatic_cancer "Pancreatic cancer") in 1996[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3) and died later that year, aged 79, on May 19, 1996 in his Los Angeles home.[[2]](#cite_note-LATimes-2)[[3]](#cite_note-NYTimes-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Hard-4)

Beradino supported [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") in the [1964 United States presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Beradino&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Filmography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/John_Beradino_and_wife_Marjorie_General_Hospital_1971..JPG/250px-John_Beradino_and_wife_Marjorie_General_Hospital_1971..JPG)](/wiki/File:John_Beradino_and_wife_Marjorie_General_Hospital_1971..JPG)

John and Marjorie Beradino, 1971.
